HomeResearchServices

Research

"How do we build a world together in a disenchanted modernity?"

My research sits at the intersection of cognitive science, cultural history, and political philosophy. I study how meaning is produced and transmitted in modern societies — and what this implies for how we organise ourselves collectively.

The common thread across my work is a single question: in a world where shared meaning has fractured — where religion, tradition, and collective institutions have lost much of their hold — how do people and organisations find coherence? And what new forms of world-making are possible?

Research Areas

Culture & Cognition

How cultural context shapes the way we think, reason, and make decisions. Drawing on cognitive science, anthropology, and psychohistory to understand the deep structures of collective thought.

Meaning in Modernity

Why meaning in contemporary Western societies is increasingly derived from individual aspirations rather than collective institutions. I use computational text analysis and psychological frameworks to trace this transition across corpora.

Collective Intelligence & Decision Systems

How groups — from organisations to political communities — can make better decisions together. Drawing on distributed cognition, deliberative democracy, and complex systems research.

Political Philosophy & Decentralisation

The normative dimension: what kinds of political structures and governance designs allow communities to remain coherent while preserving genuine plurality?

What is cognitive science? +

Created in the second half of the twentieth century, cognitive science is the interdisciplinary endeavour to study the mind as an information-processing system. It connects computer science, psychology, neuroscience, philosophy, linguistics, and anthropology. In my research, I use psychological theories and computational text analysis to address questions in philosophy and the social sciences — notably why meaning in modernity is increasingly derived from individual desires and aspirations rather than group norms and traditions.

Methods

  • Computational text analysis on large historical corpora
  • Psychohistorical and cultural analysis
  • Ethnographic fieldwork
  • Experimental and quasi-experimental design
  • Deliberative and participatory research methods

Current Work

My M.Sc. thesis at ENS Paris investigates the shift in sources of meaning in modern Western societies — from collective institutions (religion, tradition, community) toward individual aspiration — using large-scale text corpora and psychological frameworks. This work connects to broader questions about how organisations and political communities can recover shared purpose without returning to pre-modern forms of authority.
